steps.text: Provide initial briefing at the site prior to participation in any emergency response.
Include instruction in wearing appropriate personal protective equipment during the initial briefing.
Include information about chemical hazards involved during the initial briefing.
Include duties to be performed during the initial briefing.
Ensure all other appropriate safety and health precautions are used.
Specialist employees must receive training or demonstrate competency in their specialization annually.

constraints.text: Skilled support personnel exposed to hazards must receive an initial briefing.
All appropriate safety and health precautions provided to the employer’s employees must be used.

steps.text: First responders at the operations level are trained to respond in a defensive fashion without actually trying to stop the release.
First responders at the operational level shall have received at least eight hours of training or have had sufficient experience to objectively demonstrate competency.
Hazardous materials technicians shall have received at least 24 hours of training equal to the first responder operations level.

constraints.text: Know how to select and use proper personal protective equipment provided to the first responder operational level.
Competency in basic hazard and risk assessment techniques.
Understanding of basic hazardous materials terms.
Know how to perform basic control, containment and/or confinement operations.
Know how to implement basic decontamination procedures.
Understanding of relevant standard operating procedures and termination procedures.

steps.text: Review the applicable appendices to 29 CFR 1910.120.
Understand principles of toxicology and biological monitoring as they pertain to occupational health.
Know the rights and responsibilities of employees and employers under applicable OSHA and EPA laws.
Participate in hands-on exercises and demonstrations of competency with equipment, including the donning and doffing of PPE.
Utilize sources of reference, relevant manuals, and knowledge of hazard coding systems.
Complete at least 8 hours of hands-on training.
Receive training in the job skills required for an employee’s job function and responsibility before participating in or supervising field activities.
Obtain information and training prior to initial assignment into a work area.
Review the employer’s emergency response plan and procedures including first aid.
Review the employer’s hazardous waste handling procedures including materials handling program and spill containment program.
Understand the hazardous communication program meeting the requirements of 29 CFR 1910.1200.
Review the employer’s medical surveillance program and recognize signs and symptoms of exposure to hazardous substances.
Review the employer’s decontamination program and procedures.
Review the employer’s training program and responsible parties.
Review the employer’s personal protective equipment program including the proper selection and use of PPE.
Review all relevant site-specific procedures addressing potential safety and health hazards.

Review of the expected hazards including fire and explosions hazards, confined space hazards, electrical hazards, powered equipment hazards, motor vehicle hazards, and walking-working surface hazards.

Awareness and knowledge of the competencies for the First Responder at the Operations Level covered in the National Fire Protection Association’s Standard No. 472, Professional Competence of Responders to Hazardous Materials Incidents.
Review of and demonstration of competency in performing the applicable skills of 29 CFR 1910.120(q).

Hands-on experience with written and electronic information relative to response decision making including but not limited to the U.S. Department of Transportation’s Emergency Response Guidebook (ERG), manufacturer material safety data sheets, CHEMTREC/CANUTEC, shipper or manufacturer contacts, computer data bases and response models, and other relevant sources of information addressing hazardous substance releases. Familiarization with OSHA standard 29 CFR 1910.1201.

Review of the principles and practices for analyzing an incident to determine the hazardous substances present, their physical and chemical properties, the likely behavior of the hazardous substance and its container, the types of hazardous substance transportation containers and vehicles involved in the release, the appropriate strategy for approaching release sites and containing the release.

Review of procedures for implementing continuing response actions consistent with the local emergency response plan, the organization’s standard operating procedures, and the current edition of DOT’s ERG including extended emergency notification procedures and follow-up communications.
Review of the principles and practice for proper selection and use of personal protective equipment.
Institute a training program and ensure employee participation in the program.
Provide training at the time of initial assignment and at least annually thereafter.
Inform each employee of the information contained in Appendices A and B.

Inform each employee about the quantity, location, manner of use, release, or storage of AN, and the specific nature of operations which could result in exposure to AN, as well as any necessary protective steps.
Inform each employee about the purpose, proper use, and limitations of respirators and protective clothing.
Inform each employee about the purpose and a description of the medical surveillance program required by paragraph (n) of this section.
Inform each employee about the emergency procedures developed, as required by paragraph (i) of this section.
Inform each employee about engineering and work practice controls, their function, and the employee’s relationship to these controls.
Conduct a review of this standard.
Make a copy of this standard and its appendices readily available to all affected employees.
Provide, upon request, all materials relating to the employee information and training program to the Assistant Secretary and the Director.

Provide employees who are potentially exposed to EtO at or above the action level or above the excursion limit with information and training on EtO at the time of initial assignment and at least annually thereafter.
Inform employees of the requirements of this section with an explanation of its contents, including Appendices A and B.
Inform employees about any operations in their work area where EtO is present.
Inform employees about the location and availability of the written EtO final rule.
Inform employees about the medical surveillance program required by paragraph (i) of this section with an explanation of the information in Appendix C.
Inform each employee exposed to BD over the action level or STEL of the health hazards associated with BD exposure.
Inform employees of the quantity, location, manner of use, release, and storage of BD and specific operations that could result in exposure to BD.
Inform employees of the engineering controls and work practices associated with the employee’s job assignment, and emergency procedures and personal protective equipment.
Inform employees of the measures they can take to protect themselves from exposure to BD.
Provide employees with the contents of this standard and its appendices.
Inform employees of their right to obtain medical examinations and their medical records, as well as air monitoring results.
Make a copy of this standard and its appendices readily available without cost to all affected employees and their designated representatives.
Provide all training materials and information as required.
Provide ventilation in sufficient quantities to keep the concentration of vapors below 10 percent of their lower explosive limit.
Make frequent tests to ascertain the concentration of vapors.
Inspect all power and lighting cables to ensure the insulation is in excellent condition, free of all cracks and worn spots.
Ensure there are no connections within 50 feet (15.2 meters) of the operation.
Ensure lines are not overloaded.
Ensure cables are suspended with sufficient slack to prevent undue stress or chafing.
Test the atmosphere in enclosed spaces before welding, cutting or heating on metals covered by soft and greasy preservatives.

preconditions.text: Before welding, cutting or heating is commenced on any surface covered by a preservative coating whose flammability is not known, a test shall be made by a competent person to determine its flammability.

steps.text: Train employees in the applicable requirements of this section.
Train employees within 90 days of December 14, 2004, for employees currently working.
Train new employees upon initial assignment.
Train previously trained employees when necessary to maintain proficiency.
Ensure all employees are trained on emergency alarm signals.
Ensure all employees are trained on primary and secondary evacuation routes.
Ensure employees expected to fight incipient stage fires are trained on fire extinguisher use.
Ensure employees are trained on hazards associated with fixed and portable fire protection systems.
Train employees on activation and operation of fixed and portable fire protection systems.
Have a written training policy for fire response employees.
Keep written standard operating procedures for firefighting and update as necessary.
Review fire response employee training programs and hands-on sessions before use.
Provide training for fire response employees to ensure they can carry out their duties.
Provide training at no cost to the employee.
Institute a training program and ensure employee participation in the program.
Provide training prior to or at the time of initial assignment and at least annually thereafter.
Provide training for Class I operations equivalent to the EPA Model Accreditation Plan.
Provide training for Class II operations that require use of critical barriers or negative pressure enclosures.
Include all elements in paragraph (k)(9)(viii) and specific work practices for Class II work with asbestos containing materials.
Include hands-on training and at least 8 hours duration for Class II work with specific materials.
Provide training for employees working with multiple categories of material.
Provide training for Class II operations not involving specific categories of material with applicable work practices.
Train each employee involved in operating a process.
Train each employee involved in maintaining the ongoing integrity of process equipment.
Inform employees involved in operating a process and maintenance and contract employees of changes in the process.

preconditions.text: Employees shall not be permitted to participate in or supervise field activities until they have been trained to a level required by their job function and responsibility.

steps.text: Know how to implement the employer’s emergency response plan.
Know the classification, identification and verification of known and unknown materials by using field survey instruments and equipment.
Be able to function within an assigned role in the Incident Command System.
Know how to select and use proper specialized chemical personal protective equipment provided to the hazardous materials technician.
Understand hazard and risk assessment techniques.
Be able to perform advance control, containment, and/or confinement operations within the capabilities of the resources and personal protective equipment available with the unit.
Understand and implement decontamination procedures.
Understand termination procedures.
Understand basic chemical and toxicological terminology and behavior.

constraints.text: Hazardous materials technicians shall have received at least 24 hours of training equal to the first responder operations level.

preconditions.text: Skilled support personnel who have not been a part of the emergency response plan and do not meet the training requirements should be made aware of the hazards they face.

constraints.text: Skilled support personnel should have at least a safety and health briefing before entering the area of potential or actual exposure.
Skilled support personnel should be provided all necessary protective clothing and equipment required for their tasks.

steps.text: Remove electrodes from unattended electrode holders.
Ensure hot electrode holders are not dipped in water.
Open the power supply switch to the equipment when leaving work or moving the machine.
Report any faulty or defective equipment to the supervisor.
Assign additional personnel to guard against fire during welding, cutting, or heating operations.
Take precautions on both sides of walls, floors, and ceilings when performing welding, cutting, or heating.

constraints.text: Do not dip hot electrode holders in water to avoid electric shock.
Instruct personnel on specific anticipated fire hazards and use of firefighting equipment.

steps.text: Provide a training program for each employee who might be exposed to fall hazards.
Train each employee to recognize the hazards of falling.
Train each employee in the procedures to minimize fall hazards.
Assure that each employee has been trained by a competent person in various areas regarding fall hazards and protection.
Prepare a written certification record for compliance with training provisions.
Maintain the latest training certification.

A competent person shall evaluate the work area, the projected work practices and the engineering controls, and shall certify in writing, that the different or modified controls are adequate to reduce direct and indirect employee exposure to below the PELs under all expected conditions of use and that the method meets the requirements of this standard.
Before work begins and as needed during the job, a competent person shall conduct an inspection of the worksite and determine that the roofing material is intact and will likely remain intact.
All employees performing work covered by this paragraph shall be trained in a training program that meets the requirements of paragraph (k)(9)(viii) of this section.
Train each employee assigned to work on or near the equipment in how to recognize struck-by and pinch/crush hazard areas posed by the rotating superstructure.
Erect and maintain control lines, warning lines, railings or similar barriers to mark the boundaries of the hazard areas.
Train each employee to understand what the warning signs and high visibility markings signify.
